schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul makes much of this subject, setting subjects under their rulers — to show that Christ introduced his laws not for the subversion of the commonwealth but for the better ordering of it, teaching men not to take up unnecessary and unprofitable wars; let every soul be subject to the higher powers, for there is no power but of God and the powers that be are ordained of him — the duty of submission to civil government grounded in the divine institution of authority (the magistrate's power derived from God, government an ordinance for the ordering of society, Proverbs 8:15); the implied limit (obedience in things lawful, God the higher authority, Acts 5:29, 'we ought to obey God rather than men').

debates.question: Is submission to authority absolute or bounded?

sides.position: Bounded — obedience in things lawful; where the magistrate commands against God, 'we ought to obey God rather than men' (Acts 5:29), God being the higher authority

themes: let every soul be subject unto the higher powers
the powers that be are ordained of God
the divine institution of government
submission bounded by conscience toward God

schools.summary: Gill: the one who resists the power is he who denies that there ought to be such order, despises and opposes it, will not be subject in any form; Barnes: those who rise up against government itself, seeking anarchy and confusion; whoever resists the power resists the ordinance of God (for authority is God's institution, v.1), and the resisters shall receive judgment (from the magistrate and from God) — the gravity of rebellion against lawful authority (the resisting of God's ordinance); the careful sense — the rising against government itself (anarchy) condemned, not the lawful, conscientious refusal to obey a command against God (Acts 5:29).

themes: whosoever resisteth the power, resisteth the ordinance of God
they that resist shall receive damnation
the gravity of rebellion
anarchy condemned, conscience distinguished

schools.summary: Gill: Paul anticipates the objection that rulers are terrible, and removes it — governors neither do nor ought to be a terror to those who do good (in a civil sense), the well-behaved having nothing to fear; rulers are not a terror to good works but to the evil, and the one who does good shall have praise of the power — the proper function of civil government (to restrain and punish evil, and to encourage and commend the good, the magistrate the guardian of public order, fearful to the evildoer, no terror to the upright); the proper function the standard by which rulers should act (the ideal of government, though some fail it).

themes: rulers are not a terror to good works, but to the evil
do that which is good, and thou shalt have praise
the function of government
restrain evil, encourage good

schools.summary: Gill: the ruler a minister of God's appointing and commissioning, acting under him, a kind of vicegerent, appointed for the people's good (natural, moral, civil, spiritual); the ruler the minister of God to the believer for good, but a terror and avenger to the evildoer, bearing not the sword in vain — the magistrate God's servant for the public good, armed with the power of the sword (the symbol of the power of punishment, including the power over life/capital punishment, the just authority to punish), an avenger executing God's wrath on the evildoer (lawful public justice, contrasted with the believer's private forbearance, Romans 12:19).

debates.question: Does 'beareth not the sword in vain' warrant capital punishment?

sides.position: Yes — the sword is the symbol of the magistrate's power of punishment, including the power over life (capital punishment), as God's avenger on the evildoer

themes: he is the minister of God to thee for good
beareth not the sword in vain
a revenger to execute wrath
the just authority to punish

schools.summary: Barnes: the reasons for subjection two — that government is appointed by God, and that violation of the laws would expose to punishment; Gill: there is a necessity of subjection, magistracy being God's ordinance for the good of men; the believer must be subject not only for wrath (to avoid the magistrate's punishment, the prudential ground, the lower motive) but also for conscience' sake (because it is God's ordinance and conscience binds him to obey, the higher motive, conscience toward God) — the higher motive of Christian obedience (not mere fear of penalty but reverence for God who ordained the authority, the conscientious obedience).

themes: ye must needs be subject
not only for wrath, but also for conscience sake
the twofold ground
conscience toward God the higher motive

schools.summary: Gill: to show our subjection to the higher powers, and as a proof of it, we pay tribute to support them in their office and dignity, done not for fear of trouble but for conscience' sake (the payment of taxes not a mere matter of prudence but a duty); for this cause pay ye tribute also, for they are God's ministers attending continually upon this very thing — the duty of paying taxes (the support of the government that serves the public good, the rulers God's ministers devoted to their office, attending continually on the work of government), the obligation grounded in the divine appointment of the rulers.

themes: for this cause pay ye tribute also
they are God's ministers
the duty of paying taxes
the support of the government

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: we are here taught a lesson of justice and charity — to render to all their due, as a debt, not a gift; Gill: render to all princes, magistrates, and officers, from the supreme to the lowest, whatever belongs to them; render to all their dues — tribute to whom tribute (direct taxes), custom to whom custom (duties on goods), fear to whom fear (reverence), honour to whom honour (esteem), each to whom it is due — the comprehensive duty of giving everyone his due (the material dues of taxes/customs to the government, the moral dues of reverence/honour to those in authority), the justice that pays every obligation as a debt owed, not a favour given.

themes: render therefore to all their dues
tribute... custom... fear... honour
the justice that pays every obligation
dues as debts owed

schools.summary: Gill: from the payment of dues to magistrates, Paul proceeds to a general exhortation to discharge all debts — to owe no man anything, but make good all obligations, whether civil or natural; owe no man any thing but to love one another, for he that loveth another hath fulfilled the law — the duty of discharging every debt (leaving no obligation unpaid), with the one exception of love (the perpetual debt always owing, never fully discharged), and which in loving the neighbour fulfils the whole law toward him (the second table comprehended in love); 'owe no man anything' not forbidding lawful borrowing/credit but the leaving of debts unpaid.

debates.question: Does 'owe no man any thing' forbid all borrowing or credit?

sides.position: No — it forbids leaving debts unpaid and failing obligations, not lawful borrowing/credit; the duty is to discharge debts when due

themes: owe no man any thing, but to love one another
he that loveth another hath fulfilled the law
the perpetual debt of love
love the fulfilling of the law

schools.summary: Gill: Paul reckons up the laws of the second table (toward the neighbour), to show that so far as a man loves his neighbour he fulfils the law; the commandments — against adultery, killing, stealing, false witness, coveting — all comprehended in the one saying 'Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself' (the second table of the law comprehended in the one command of love, for love does no wrong to the neighbour and all good); love the sum of the law toward the neighbour (the one principle fulfilling all the particular commands), the standard 'as thyself'; Paul omitting the first table here (the context the duty to others, the love to God assumed).

themes: Thou shalt not commit adultery... kill... steal
comprehended in... Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself
the second table summed in love
love the sum of the law

schools.summary: Gill: the man who truly loves his neighbour will contrive no ill against him nor do any to him — not injure his person, defile his bed, defraud his substance, hurt his character, or covet his goods, but do him all the good he can; love worketh no ill to his neighbour, therefore love is the fulfilling of the law — since love does no wrong to the neighbour (the negative duties) and all good (the positive), it accomplishes all the law requires (the love fulfilling the law); love the principle behind the commands (the commandments forbidding the wrongs love would never do, requiring the goods love delights to render), the spring of obedience.

themes: love worketh no ill to his neighbour
love is the fulfilling of the law
no ill and all good
love the principle that does the law

schools.summary: Chrysostom: having given the fitting commands, Paul again thrusts them on to good works in consideration of what is pressing — the time of judgment at the doors (as he wrote to the Corinthians, 'the remaining time is short,' 1 Corinthians 7:29, and to the Hebrews, 'yet a little while'); knowing the time, that now it is high time to awake out of sleep (the spiritual drowsiness, sloth, carnal security), for now is our salvation (the full, final salvation, the consummation) nearer than when we believed — the motive of the shortness of time and the nearness of the consummation, the call to rouse from drowsiness to vigilance and diligence (the urgency of the redeemed life, Ephesians 5:16).

themes: knowing the time... high time to awake out of sleep
our salvation nearer than when we believed
the shortness of time
the urgency of the redeemed life

schools.summary: Gill: 'the night' best understood of the present time of life (called by the Jews 'this world is like to the night'), the day the coming glory; the night far spent, the day at hand — let us cast off the works of darkness (the deeds of sin, the carnal practices, the unfruitful works) and put on the armour of light (the graces, the holy life, the defenses of righteousness, Ephesians 6:11) — the call to lay aside darkness and put on light as those who belong to the coming day (the children of light), the urgency of the figure (the night nearly gone, the day at hand, the time to rise).

themes: the night is far spent, the day is at hand
cast off the works of darkness
put on the armour of light
belonging to the coming day

schools.summary: Gill: being under the day of the gospel, with the daystar risen in our hearts and exposed to the view of all in broad daylight, we ought to walk decently, not naked but clothed with the armour of light; let us walk honestly (decently, becomingly) as in the day, not in rioting and drunkenness (intemperance, excess), not in chambering and wantonness (impurity, uncleanness), not in strife and envying (contention, discord) — the honest, holy walk that befits the children of the day (the six works of darkness in three pairs/categories — intemperance, impurity, contention — to be shunned, the deeds of the night against the walk of the day).

themes: let us walk honestly, as in the day
not in rioting and drunkenness... chambering... strife
intemperance, impurity, contention shunned
the walk that befits the day

schools.summary: Gill: putting on the Lord Jesus Christ as a man puts on his clothes — his righteousness (the best robe, covering and adorning, for justification) and also Christ in the daily walk (in imitation and the exercise of grace, for sanctification); put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh to fulfil its lusts — the positive (putting on Christ, his righteousness and likeness) and the negative (giving the flesh no place, occasion, or fuel, the lusts denied) of the redeemed life; the verse of Augustine's conversion (read in the garden, piercing him and turning him from his sins to Christ, the power of the word).

themes: put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ
make not provision for the flesh, to fulfil the lusts
the positive and negative of the redeemed life
the verse of Augustine's conversion
